Summary: Added typing-time input restrictions to the Personal Information form so invalid characters are blocked as the user types. Name-like fields now reject digits, phone fields reject letters, postal code accepts digits only, identifier fields accept digits/hyphens, and address fields allow practical alphanumeric address characters. A focused widget test was added to prove the blocking behavior, and the existing personal-information screen test still passes.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Personal Information form now uses inputFormatters to block invalid typed characters by field type, not just submit-time validation.
2. [finished] Name-like fields in the profile form reject digits; phone fields reject letters; postal code is digits only; identifier fields accept only digits and hyphens.
3. [finished] Address fields in the profile form allow practical alphanumeric address characters so street numbers remain usable.
4. [finished] A new widget test verifies that invalid typed characters are filtered from first name, phone number, PhilHealth ID, and postal code fields.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Added input formatter support and field-specific typing restrictions to prevent invalid characters from being entered in the Personal Information form. | Notes: Also added helper formatter functions for names, phone numbers, identifiers, addresses, and birth date.
2. [created] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget_test.dart — Added a focused widget test that verifies invalid characters are blocked as the user types into multiple profile fields. | Notes: Test covers first name, phone number, PhilHealth ID, and postal code.

Summary: Implemented stricter field-type validation for the Personal Information flow. The frontend profile form now rejects digits in text fields and letters in numeric fields through validators, and the backend DTOs enforce the same rules for names, communication language, city/province/country, optional personal detail text fields, phone numbers, postal code, PhilHealth/PhilSys IDs, and emergency contact phone. Address lines remain mixed alphanumeric for practical street-address support. Focused backend DTO tests and the Flutter Personal Information screen test passed, and the Kanban card was moved to done.

Facts:
1. [finished] Text fields in the Personal Information form now reject numeric characters, and numeric fields reject alphabetic characters, with validation shown before save.
2. [finished] Backend DTO validation now mirrors the frontend for names, communication language, city/province/country, optional personal detail text fields, phone numbers, postal code, identifiers, and emergency contact phone.
3. [finished] Address line fields intentionally allow letters, numbers, and basic punctuation so real street addresses with house numbers remain valid.
4. [finished] Focused Jest and Flutter tests for the Personal Information validation flow passed after the changes.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/widgets/patient_profile_form_widget.dart — Added and tightened validators for text-only and numeric-only profile fields in the Personal Information form. | Notes: Address fields use a separate mixed alphanumeric rule.
2. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Added coverage that validates the form rejects digits in text fields and letters in phone-number fields. | Notes: The test now uses FormState.validate() directly for stability.
3. [modified] backend/src/auth/dto/profile-name.dto.ts — Retains strict name validation so name fields reject digits and malformed text. | Notes: Already part of the profile validation boundary.
4. [modified] backend/src/auth/dto/patient-profile.dto.ts — Added backend regex constraints for text-only and numeric-only profile fields to match the frontend behavior. | Notes: Includes communication language, city/province/country, identifiers, postal code, and emergency contact phone.
5. [modified] backend/src/auth/dto/patient-profile.dto.spec.ts — Added backend DTO test coverage for rejecting digits in text fields and letters in numeric fields. | Notes: Confirms the authoritative API-side enforcement.

Summary: Implemented field-format validation for the Personal Information profile flow on both frontend and backend. The frontend profile form now validates text-like fields, phone numbers, postal code, and PhilHealth/PhilSys IDs before save. The backend DTOs now enforce matching rules for names, phone numbers, postal code, and identifier formats while still allowing either PhilHealth or PhilSys to satisfy the identifier requirement. Focused backend and Flutter tests passed, and the Kanban card was moved to done.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Personal Information form now validates name-like fields, phone numbers, postal code, and identifier formats before submitting.
2. [finished] Backend profile DTOs now enforce format rules for names, phone numbers, postal code, PhilHealth ID, and PhilSys ID.
3. [finished] Either PhilHealth ID or PhilSys ID still satisfies the profile identifier requirement; both are no longer required.
4. [finished] Focused backend Jest tests and Flutter profile tests passed after the validation changes.

Affected files:

content:
Summary: Implemented Phase 2 of the interoperability rollout across backend and Flutter. The backend now exposes a WAH4PC-backed interoperability proxy for provider discovery and sync-request preparation, with env validation and e2e coverage. The Flutter app now launches a dedicated sync wizard from the Personal Information screen, uses bottom-sheet selection for identifier/provider choice, and reuses the existing profile readiness rules to gate entry. Tests for the new interoperability models, wizard flow, and profile-to-wizard navigation all pass.

Facts:
1. [finished] The backend interoperability proxy now exposes GET /api/v1/interoperability/providers and POST /api/v1/interoperability/sync/prepare, backed by the real WAH4PC gateway endpoint wah4pc.echosphere.cfd.
2. [finished] The backend environment contract now requires WAH4PC_GATEWAY_URL, WAH4PC_API_KEY, and WAH4PC_PROVIDER_ID, and those values are documented in backend/.env.example and backend/README.md.
3. [finished] The Flutter sync flow now launches a dedicated SyncRecordsWizardScreen from the Personal Information sync button, instead of showing only a placeholder snackbar.
4. [finished] The Flutter wizard uses existing bottom-sheet selection controls to pick a PhilHealth or PhilSys identifier and then an active provider, while preserving profile readiness gating from Phase 1.
5. [finished] A Flutter test harness file was added to mock flutter_secure_storage calls globally so AuthSession.clear() can run in widget tests without a plugin exception.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/app.module.ts — Registered the new interoperability integration module with the Nest application so provider discovery endpoints are available. | Notes: Added IntegrationModule import and module registration.
2. [modified] backend/src/common/config/env.validation.ts — Added required gateway environment variables to startup validation for the backend proxy. | Notes: Now validates WAH4PC_GATEWAY_URL, WAH4PC_API_KEY, and WAH4PC_PROVIDER_ID.
3. [created] backend/src/integration/integration.module.ts — Created a dedicated Nest module to encapsulate interoperability gateway concerns. | Notes: Contains the new integration controller and service.

6. [created] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Defines the typed gateway provider and sync-preparation response contracts used by the new integration layer. | Notes: Keeps request and response shapes explicit.
7. [created] backend/src/integration/dto/prepare-sync-request.dto.ts — Validates inbound sync preparation requests at the backend boundary. | Notes: Uses class-validator for provider and identifier fields.

Summary: Reviewed the interoperability rollout docs and backlog to shape Phase 2. The intended implementation boundary is a new sync wizard started from Personal Information, with identifier selection and provider selection handled as separate steps. Provider discovery and readiness validation should live in a dedicated interoperability module rather than inside the profile screen. The backlog item for Phase 2 was updated to reflect this narrowed scope.

Facts:
1. [finished] Phase 2 should start from the Personal Information sync action and open a dedicated multi-step sync wizard rather than adding provider selection directly into the profile editor.
2. [finished] Identifier selection for the sync flow should remain compatible with PhilHealth ID or PhilSys ID, and the selection UX can use a bottom sheet inside the wizard.
3. [finished] Provider discovery is documented as a gateway/backend concern, so frontend code should consume a backend provider endpoint instead of hardcoding provider options.

Affected files:

3. [inspected] wah4pc.md — Used to confirm the gateway provider discovery and FHIR request endpoints that phase 2 must respect. | Notes: Provides endpoint and payload guidance.
4. [inspected] backend/src/app.module.ts — Used to verify how a new interoperability module would be registered in the backend application. | Notes: Shows current Nest module composition.
5. [inspected] backend/src/common/config/env.validation.ts — Used to verify how new gateway-related environment variables should be validated. | Notes: Current env schema does not yet include gateway variables.
6. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Used to see how the current sync button is wired and where the new route should be introduced. | Notes: Current screen still contains the Phase 1 readiness card and placeholder sync action.
7. [inspected] frontend/lib/core/widgets/ui/inputs/bottom_sheet_select_form_field.dart — Used to confirm there is an existing bottom-sheet select control that can be reused for identifier/provider selection. | Notes: Good fit for wizard step selection.
8. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/appointments/presentation/screens/appointment_booking_screen.dart — Used as a pattern reference for multi-step screen orchestration in Flutter. | Notes: Useful for the proposed wizard screen structure.

Summary: Removed PhilSys ID from the backend profile contract and aligned the frontend shared profile model with the trimmed response shape. The backend now no longer validates, stores, returns, or includes philSysId in auth/profile flows, and a SQL migration was added to strip existing philSysId keys from profiles.patient_profile JSONB rows. The frontend profile model and tests were updated to remove the stale field, while the Personal Information screen continues to rely on PhilHealth ID only. Backend build and targeted frontend Flutter tests passed after the change.

Facts:
1. [finished] Backend auth/profile flow no longer contains philSysId in the DTO, response contract, repository mapping, or login fallback profile snapshot.
2. [finished] The profiles.patient_profile JSONB data now has a migration at sqls/010_remove_philsys_id_from_profiles.sql that removes existing philSysId keys from stored rows.
3. [finished] Frontend shared profile models were trimmed to remove philSysId so the app contract matches the backend response shape.
4. [finished] Targeted Flutter tests for profile readiness and the Personal Information screen passed after the contract cleanup, and the backend Nest build succeeded.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/auth/auth.types.ts — Removed philSysId from the backend patient profile response contract so the API no longer exposes the field. | Notes: This is the canonical backend response shape used by login/profile flows.

4. [modified] backend/src/auth/profile.repository.ts — Removed philSysId from profile normalization, response mapping, and missing-field checks so stored profile snapshots no longer track the field. | Notes: The JSONB profile record is now read/written without philSysId.
5. [modified] backend/src/auth/login-auth.service.ts — Removed philSysId from the empty profile fallback returned during auth/profile resolution failures. | Notes: Prevents stale backend fallback payloads from exposing the field.
6. [created] sqls/010_remove_philsys_id_from_profiles.sql — Adds the migration that cleans existing persisted profile JSONB rows by removing philSysId keys. | Notes: This is the database-side cleanup for existing data.
7. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/models/auth_api_models.dart — Trimmed the frontend shared profile model to remove the stale philSysId property and JSON serialization/parsing. | Notes: Keeps the app contract aligned with the backend response shape.

content:
Summary: Implemented Phase 1 of the interoperability rollout on the Flutter frontend by adding a reusable profile sync-readiness helper, gating the Personal Information screen sync action behind profile completeness plus at least one identifier, and relaxing identifier inputs so PhilHealth ID and PhilSys ID are optional individually while sync requires one of them. Added tests for the readiness helper and updated the Personal Information screen test to cover the locked state and disabled sync button. The phase 1 Kanban card was moved from backlog to in-progress and then to done after validation.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Personal Information screen now shows a sync-readiness card that reflects whether the profile is ready for interoperability sync and lists missing requirements when it is not ready.
2. [finished] Sync records are gated by profile completion plus at least one identifier; the helper treats PhilHealth ID or PhilSys ID as sufficient for sync readiness.
3. [finished] PhilHealth ID and PhilSys ID fields in the profile form are now optional inputs with explanatory copy instead of required fields, so the user can save profile changes without both identifiers filled in.
4. [finished] A pure profile sync-readiness helper was added under frontend/lib/features/profile/domain/profile_sync_readiness.dart for reuse and testability.
5. [finished] New tests cover readiness evaluation and the Personal Information screen locked state; targeted Flutter tests passed after the changes.

Affected files:

No create/receive endpoints were added, matching the user's request to handle creation later. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

1. [finished] Health Records now uses four dedicated Supabase tables: medical_history_records, immunization_records, medical_consultation_records, and laboratory_result_records.

3. [finished] Flutter Health Records screens now use HealthRecordDataScreen to load entries through HealthRecordsApiClient with the existing bearer token from AuthSession.

Summary: Implemented read-only Supabase-backed Health Records storage integration. Added SQL schema for a single health_records table designed for manual Supabase inserts and future backend create endpoints, added backend database typings and a read-only NestJS HealthRecords module, and refactored Flutter Health Records screens to load authenticated records from the backend without adding any create/receive endpoints. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

Facts:
1. [finished] Health Records now use a new Supabase table named public.health_records with section_key, title/subtitle, summary fields, filter/status fields, status/accent tone keys, icon_key, details_json, display_order, recorded_at, created_at, and updated_at.
2. [finished] The backend exposes a read-only GET /health-records?section=<section_key> endpoint protected by the existing API key guard and bearer access-token user resolution; no POST/create endpoint was added.
3. [finished] The Flutter Health Records screens no longer contain hardcoded HealthRecordEntry sample data; they use a shared HealthRecordSectionScreen that loads records by section and maps API responses into the existing HealthRecordScreenTemplate.
4. [finished] Manual Supabase inserts should use section_key values medical_history, immunization_records, medical_consultations, or laboratory_results and details_json as an array of objects with label and value string fields.

Affected files:
1. [created] sqls/007_health_records_setup.sql — Defines the new Supabase Health Records table, constraints, indexes, trigger, and RLS policies for manual storage and future backend integration. | Notes: Must be applied to Supabase before live reads can return seeded data.
2. [modified] backend/src/supabase/database.types.ts — Adds typed Database metadata for the health_records table and a Json type used by details_json. | Notes: Keeps backend Supabase reads type-safe.
3. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.types.ts — Defines Health Records backend response contracts, section key validation, tone types, and details_json parsing. | Notes: No create input types were added.
4. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.repository.ts — Implements read-only Supabase querying and row-to-response mapping for Health Records. | Notes: Orders by display_order ascending then recorded_at descending.
5. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.service.ts — Owns auth-token user resolution and section-key validation before delegating reads to the repository. | Notes: Uses existing AuthSupportService.
6. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.controller.ts — Provides the GET /health-records endpoint with query-based section selection. | Notes: No receiving/create endpoint was added.
7. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.module.ts — Registers the Health Records controller, service, repository, AuthModule, and SupabaseModule. | Notes: Dedicated module preserves domain separation.
8. [modified] backend/src/app.module.ts — Imports and registers HealthRecordsModule in the NestJS application. | Notes: Keeps endpoint active in the backend app.
9. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/data/health_records_api_client.dart — Adds a typed Flutter API client for read-only Health Records list requests using existing backend config and bearer-token auth. | Notes: Handles timeout/backend errors and response parsing.
10. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/mappers/health_record_entry_mapper.dart — Maps backend HealthRecordResponse objects to existing HealthRecordEntry presentation models, including tone and icon key mapping. | Notes: Keeps API parsing separate from widget rendering.
11. Summary: Reviewed the frontend Health Records screens and the existing Personal Records architecture to plan a future local persistence layer for Health Records. The current Health Records feature is still presentation-driven with hardcoded entry data in the screen files, while Personal Records already has a dedicated typed data/repository pattern. content:
Summary: Added a plain-English atlas for WAH4P so future work can read the project like a map. The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. This memory also clarifies which screens are static, which are route-driven, and which backend APIs support each major journey.

Facts:
1. [finished] Think of the repository as three layers: frontend/ is the Flutter presentation and navigation layer, backend/ is the NestJS security and business-logic layer, and sqls/ plus the data-structure docs define the Supabase/Postgres persistence layer.
2. [finished] The app begins at the Flutter shell in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, which owns theme setup, route generation, startup gating, and resume-time MPIN locking; the visible launch flow is controlled from there rather than from individual screens.
3. [finished] Startup behavior is intentionally simple to follow: the app checks whether a valid session exists, whether onboarding is complete, and whether MPIN unlock is required, then routes the user to onboarding, login, dashboard, or the MPIN unlock screen.
4. [finished] The Flutter route system is centralized in frontend/lib/app/app_routes.dart and frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart, so route names and screen mappings should be edited there first when changing navigation.
5. [finished] The route map forms a clear product atlas: splash and onboarding lead into registration and login, then into the dashboard, and from there into health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, security settings, and legal/privacy screens.
6. [finished] The frontend feature folders mirror product domains, with separate areas for auth, onboarding, dashboard, health_records, phr, appointments, calendar, medication_resupply, profile, notification, legal, and splash.
7. [finished] The auth feature is one of the largest frontend domains and includes the registration screens, login, email verification, forgot password, MPIN setup/unlock, TOTP challenge/setup, and security verification flows.
8. [finished] The backend app module in backend/src/app.module.ts wires configuration, throttling, the health module, and the auth module, while global guards enforce API-key access and rate limiting across the server.
9. [finished] The backend health endpoint is intentionally public and small, while the rest of the server is protected unless a route is marked public with the custom decorator.
10. [finished] The backend auth module is the server-side workflow hub and splits responsibilities into registration, login, password reset, profile, TOTP, MPIN, and support services/repositories instead of one monolithic auth implementation.
11. [finished] The backend auth controller groups the API by user journey: registration OTP request/verify/complete, password reset request/verify/complete, login and refresh, TOTP setup and verification, security verification, MPIN setup/verification/device registration, and device unregister flow.
12. [finished] The backend profile controller is separate from the login flow and handles authenticated profile retrieval and updates via /profile/me, which matches the frontend profile and personal information screens.
13. [finished] The project’s data rules are to keep canonical patient data in Supabase/Postgres, keep session/onboarding/device flags in secure local storage, and treat dashboard summaries and similar UI aggregates as derived data rather than separate source records.
14. [finished] Some documentation files are secondary references rather than the live truth, especially the older screen inventory; for navigation work, the live Flutter route constants and route builder are the authoritative map.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\wah4p_app.dart — The app shell controls theme, startup gating, and resume-time locking, making it the top-level navigation anchor for the project map. | Notes: Central entry point for the Flutter client.

content:
Summary: Saved a deeper project map for WAH4P that focuses on navigation, feature boundaries, and service responsibilities. The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. The frontend route tree now has a precise map from startup through onboarding, auth, dashboard, PHR, EHR-style records, appointments, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. The backend API surface is also clearer: a public health check plus a broad auth/profile domain that covers registration, login, 2FA, MPIN, password reset, and profile hydration/update. This memory layer is intended to function as an app map, not just a summary.

Facts:
1. [finished] The Flutter client has a single centralized app shell in frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart, and that shell owns the Material theme, navigator key, route generation, startup route wiring, and resume-time MPIN lock behavior.
2. [finished] The app startup path is explicit: frontend/lib/app/app_startup_service.dart resolves the first route based on refreshed auth session, onboarding completion, and MPIN device state, returning onboarding, login, dashboard, or MPIN unlock.
3. [finished] The startup gate screen in frontend/lib/app/startup_gate_screen.dart is only a transient decision screen; it shows loading/status text, then immediately replaces itself with the resolved initial route.
4. [finished] The app lock policy is device-state driven: frontend/lib/app/app_lock_state_service.dart marks the app backgrounded and requires unlock after a five-minute timeout on resume when the user is authenticated.
5. [finished] The route registry is centralized in frontend/lib/app/app_routes.dart, and the route builder in frontend/lib/app/app_router.dart maps every visible screen to a named route with mostly fade, slide, or instant transitions.
6. [finished] The Flutter route map covers these major user journey groups: splash/onboarding, registration, login and MFA, dashboard, health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, security settings, and legal/privacy.
7. [finished] The frontend feature tree is organized by responsibility under frontend/lib/features/, with dedicated folders for auth, dashboard, health_records, medication_resupply, phr, profile, onboarding, splash, calendar, notification, legal, and appointments.
8. [finished] The backend application module in backend/src/app.module.ts wires configuration, throttling, the auth module, and the health module, while also installing global guards for API-key authorization and rate limiting.
9. [finished] The backend public surface is intentionally small: backend/src/health/health.controller.ts exposes a public GET /health endpoint, while all other routes are protected by the API-key guard unless explicitly marked public.
10. [finished] The backend auth module in backend/src/auth/auth.module.ts is the main domain module and composes controllers plus specialized services for registration, login, password reset, TOTP, MPIN, profile, and supporting repositories.
11. [finished] The backend auth controller groups the API into clear workflow families: registration OTP flow, password reset flow, login and refresh, TOTP setup/challenge, security verification, MPIN set/verify/register/unregister, and device/profile-related auth support.
12. [finished] The backend profile controller in backend/src/auth/profile.controller.ts is separate from the auth workflow endpoints and provides /profile/me GET and PATCH handlers for authenticated profile hydration and updates.
13. [finished] The project docs still matter as design references: data-structure.md defines Supabase/Postgres as the system of record and local secure storage for session/onboarding/device flags, while checklist.md tracks unfinished persistence and validation work.
14. [finished] The current source tree and the older screen inventory docs are not perfectly aligned, so the live Flutter route map should be treated as the authoritative reference for actual navigation until the docs are reconciled.

Affected files:

content:
Summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers. I also captured the current testing surface and a couple of documentation mismatches that future work should reconcile.

Facts:
1. [finished] The repository is organized as a multi-part WAH4P project with backend/, frontend/, sqls/, and documentation files at the root, so future changes should treat it as a full-stack product rather than a single app.
2. [finished] The backend is a NestJS API under backend/ that uses Supabase for auth/data, Resend for email OTP delivery, global API-key protection, throttling, and strict Joi-based environment validation.
3. [finished] The backend HTTP entrypoint sets the global API prefix to /api/v1, enables helmet, applies a whitelist/transform ValidationPipe, and reads CORS origins from FRONTEND_ORIGIN.
4. [finished] The frontend is a Flutter app under frontend/ with a centralized Material theme, Open Sans typography, secure storage, and a startup gate flow driven by app state and route generation.
5. [finished] frontend/lib/app/wah4p_app.dart manages app lifecycle behavior, including MPIN lock handling on resume by checking local device state and backend security status before showing the unlock route.
6. [finished] Project docs in data-structure.md and checklist.md define Supabase/Postgres as the canonical source of truth for patient data, while local storage is reserved for session, onboarding, and device-scoped flags.
7. [finished] The repo has active test coverage in both layers: backend has Jest unit/e2e scaffolding, and frontend has multiple widget/screen tests under frontend/test.
8. [finished] SCREENS.md and some docs appear to reflect an older source tree or source-of-truth path, so documentation should be reconciled with the current repository layout before relying on it as the only inventory.

Affected files:
